text: The video shows a laparoscopic detorsion procedure of an ilioanal J pouch, with the surgeon using laparoscopic instruments to carefully untwist the pouch structure.

Restorative proctocolectomy with ilial pouch anal anastomosis or IPAA, which was first introduced in nineteen seventy eight, has been the surgical treatment of choice for patients with ulcerative colitis who are medically refractory or in those patients with dysplasia.
Because of the widespread adoption of laparoscopic surgery, the outcomes for this procedure have been proven to be better than those with the open technique.
Even though the technique has improved, complications are still present and can include small bowel obstruction, pouch leakage, anastomotic stricture, fistula formation, and pouchitis, to name a few.
The case presented in this video is of a thirty-seven-year-old female with a history of ulcerative colitis who underwent a three-stage J-pouch procedure one year prior.
She presented to the emergency department with abdominal distension, nausea, dull abdominal pain, and inability to pass stool or gas for one day's duration.
Of note, the patient underwent stricture dilation two weeks ago.
Upon admission, a rectal tube was placed with relief of her pain and distension.
The suspicion was still high for an obstruction at the level of the pouch, so the patient was taken for a diagnostic laparoscopy.
The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy, revealing twisting of the pouch upon entry into the ilioanal canal, prompting the initiation of a diagnostic laparoscopy.
Upon entry into the abdomen, the surgeon observes clear free fluid and notes the dilation of the small bowel extending to the pelvis.
The surgeon visualizes the pouch and lifts the uterus, revealing a 360-degree twist of the uterus and its mesentery.
The surgeon visually assesses the bowel, noting that it appears uncompromised, with no signs of ischemia or damage.
An additional trocar is placed to allow the assistant surgeon to help with grasping. The team gently manipulates and successfully detorses the twisted J pouch, restoring its normal orientation.
The surgeon observes the J-pouch in its correct positioning with no visible twisting, under good exposure within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes the mesentery, confirming there is no intrinsic twisting present, while examining the abdominal cavity.
The surgical team examines the deeper pelvic region to verify the correct positioning of the anatomical structures, ensuring no twisting or misalignment is present.
The surgeon identifies adhesions between the distal portion of the J-pouch and the pelvis, and proceeds to sharply lyse these adhesions using surgical instruments.
The surgeon observes a large empty presacral space, which may have contributed to the twisting of the J-pouch.
After detorsion of the J-pouch, the surgeon runs the small bowel using gentle grasping techniques, inspecting the bowel for any abnormalities or obstructions.
The surgeon visually confirms the absence of additional points of obstruction in the surgical field, ensuring clear passage within the ileorectal anastomosis area.
The surgeon identifies the transition point between the dilated and collapsed sections of the bowel, visually distinguishing the change in bowel diameter.
The surgeon returns the J-pouch to its neutral position in the pelvis after running the length of the bowel, ensuring proper anatomical alignment.
The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy to visually confirm that the J-pouch is properly detorsed, inspecting the interior of the pouch for any signs of twisting or obstruction.

The surgeon observes the mucosa, which appears unremarkable, and identifies slight stricturing at the anastomosis site. The strictured area is gently dilated to facilitate the passage of the sigmoidoscope.
The surgeon desufflates the J-pouch and begins performing a pexy, securing the J-pouch to the pelvic sidewall to minimize future torsion risk.
The surgeon takes seromuscular bites of the J-pouch and superficial bites of the pelvic sidewall, tying them together with multiple throws before cutting the suture.
The surgeon repeats the process on the patient's right side, carefully positioning the J-pouch without applying excessive tension to ensure proper placement and minimize the risk of torsion.
The surgeon places two additional sutures superior to the previously placed ones on the J-pouch, ensuring reinforcement while carefully avoiding underlying vascular structures.
The surgeon ties down the sutures and assesses the orientation and laxity of the J-pouch, carefully lifting the uterus to evaluate the distal orientation and traction of the pouch.
The surgeon places two pelvic drains in the surgical site, removes the trocars, and completes the ileorectal anastomosis procedure.
The patient recovered uneventfully over the next several days with return of bowel function and was discharged home.
When formulating a differential diagnosis in a patient with a J-pouch with outflow obstruction, you have to keep several key diagnoses in mind.
J-pouch kinking or torsion, ilioanal stricture, intra-abdominal adhesions, and afferent loop obstruction.
In this patient, we were able to rule out other pathologies and diagnose her with torsion.
Laparoscopy in our patient proved to be an excellent diagnostic and therapeutic tool.

In conclusion, J-pouch torsion is an event that can be completely addressed laparoscopically with ease, and one should always be suspicious of torsion as a diagnosis if they present repeatedly for an anastomotic stricture dilation, but with continued outflow obstruction soon after.
